title: The String Type
description: Store and manipulate text in TypeScript.

slides.title: Working with Text 📝
slides.content:
Just like in JavaScript, any text data in TypeScript uses the `string` type.

Whether it's a single character or a massive paragraph, it's always just a `string`.

slides.title: String Syntax
slides.content: You can declare strings using single quotes `''`, double quotes `""`, or backticks ```` (for template strings).

quizData.question: Which of these strings allows you to inject variables using `${variable}` syntax?
quizData.explanation: Backticks allow for 'template strings', where you can directly inject variables or expressions.

slides.title: IntelliSense Magic ✨
slides.content:
Because we typed `firstName` as a `string`, if we type `firstName.` in our editor, TypeScript will immediately show us string-specific methods.

It will suggest `.toUpperCase()`, `.substring()`, `.length`, etc., but it will completely hide number-specific methods like `.toFixed()`.

slides.title: Preventing Errors
slides.content: Try assigning a number to your string variable:
slides.codeSnippet:
let color: string = "red";
color = 5; // ERROR: Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'.
